> `reflect.Type` and pass `reflect.TypeOf(ComponentGetMessageResultOutput{})` comes to mind as an option.

Yep, certainly an option. It's a more primitive type and may reduce some confusion around "Why am I passing an `Output` here?" On the other hand, then they have to write `reflect.TypeOf(ComponentGetMessageResultOutput{})` when we could just do that for them. And someone may end up passing some other `reflect.Type` completely unrelated to an output.

I think I'll stick with what I've got as it forces passing an output. Users aren't typically going to be using `Call` directly anyway (instead they'll be using generated methods/functions in our SDKs).

> For `self Resource`, is only the URN/id actually used?

It'll be used as the `__self__` argument, which is marshaled as a resource reference (which ends up using the URN/id). It's also used for the provider/version options, if they're available on the resource.

Sorry for the late response, and thanks for the continuous effort to push this forward.
Unfortunately, I haven't had a chance to take a deep look, but you are changing the permissions on a lot more stages than only for the `make init` stage (as discussed above).
This is quite a bullet. It could be dangerous (since it just sets 777 on basically everything) and is only a part of the solution (since LocalStack is not tested to be executed as a non-root user, which means that some tasks will fail).
It also seems that some of the might not be necessary (f.e. on `/usr/lib/localstack`).

However, most (or all) of these commands are necessary. But since there are so many of them, I think a more fine-grained approach would be better. This could be done by creating the `localstack` user earlier in the `Dockerfile` (but at least in the base stage) and setting the user and the root group for `ADD` and `COPY` commands. This would then respect the permissions right away. This would also be a more robust approach considering future additions / changes of the `Dockerfile` since there won't be an automated OpenShift testing stage in the near future.

I hope you can agree - at least to a certain degree - and I am happy to discuss this further.

The `optional` keyword was used in JSONSchema v2 ([draft 2 specification](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-zyp-json-schema-02)), but is not present in v4 ([draft 4 specification](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-fge-json-schema-validation-00)). The [changelog of v3](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-zyp-json-schema-03#anchor54) shows how they `Replaced "optional" attribute with "required" attribute`. Since the specified JSONSchema for the reana.yaml schema is draft 4 (at the beginning of this file), maybe we can remove the line with `"optional"`, even if it does no harm.
We can maybe also remove it from the rest of the file, since it may be misleading...

Whenever we use s.update() we have to cast/control some of the parameters (the Spectrum could have been created by any other code), when it's right out of the Factory then the format/units are under control.

Computing only abscoeff in eq_spectrum() may actually be faster even if you wanted to plot a transmittance eventually. For sure you'll have to recompute the transmittance with some unnecessary unit/format checks, but at least you won't waste time and memory computing and generating arrays for radiance, emissivity, etc. And won't spend time applying a slot function to the radiance neither.

This is a good question. I've been bitten hard by cancelation causing me to think I've succeeded leading to UB (https://github.com/oneapi-src/oneTBB/issues/253). That said, the Committee has decided that `std::stop_token` shouldn't use exceptions for cancelation (early versions of `std::stop_token` had a `throw_if_canceled` member function). OTOH, `std::call_once` either succeeds or throws; there's no other way out. We could have it be
```
template <typename Fn>
[[nodiscard]] task_group_status collaborative_call_once(collaborative_once_flag& flag, Fn&& fn);
```
Then if client code wants it to throw on cancelation, we can easily wrap it. But if we do that, please please make it `[[nodiscard]]`!

I'm trying to wrap my head around the general pattern of what we're doing, correct me if I'm wrong. Right now there are three sets of predicates:
1. all the available predicates from the data model
2. A subset of 1, all the predicates that the BlockLearners use as candidates.
3. A subset of 2, the final predicates chosen by the BlockLearners that we use to actually do the blocking in Dedupe.pairs().

Right now BlockLearners are handed 1, and it filters that down to 2 using whether or not predicates are index predicates or not, using the `index_predicates` flag. Then it does the step of filtering from 2 to 3.

Raylet has handled this I think. But the error is because of a bug right? What we should do is:

- wait until send_reply succeeded
- exit in the process

There are two places where the error could be:
1. Raylet failed to send: in this case, raylet remove this worker and this worker will be leaked.
2. core worker failed to reply: in this case, 1) raylet remove this worker; 2) this worker will exit on itself.

If we don't ensure the ordering here, there will be a race condition: a) the worker is sending reply; b) the worker is exiting. 

If b) finished first, a) will return an error to raylet. With the current implementation, it'll go through 2) mentioned above.

But this actually is not an error and enforce ordering will make it work, then why don't we just avoid it and the error msg in raylet will be more accurate.

Yes, a single resolve should be used for "an entire compilation graph", and it should be selected at the root before that compile is kicked off.

Imagining a situation where you have different resolves for two root targets (`deploy_jar`s for example): even though those binaries might depend on the same inner nodes in the graph, they will each trigger compilation with different resolves. That will mean that the compiles for those distinct resolves will happen independently and in parallel (since the resolve that is being used is part of the request, and thus part of the `@rule`'s identity.)

```suggestion
async def race_condition(offset: float = 0) -> List[str]:
    """Common code for below race condition tests."""

    async def test_task(deadline: float, loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop) -> None:
        # We need the internal Timeout class to specify the deadline (not delay).
        # This is needed to create the precise timing to reproduce the race condition.
        with pytest.warns(DeprecationWarning):
            with Timeout(deadline, loop):
                await asyncio.sleep(10)

    call_order: List[str] = []
    f_exit = log_func(Timeout._do_exit, "exit", call_order)
    Timeout._do_exit = f_exit  # type: ignore[assignment]
    f_timeout = log_func(Timeout._on_timeout, "timeout", call_order)
    Timeout._on_timeout = f_timeout  # type: ignore[assignment]

    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    deadline = loop.time() + 1
    t = asyncio.create_task(test_task(deadline, loop))
    loop.call_at(deadline + offset, log_func(t.cancel, "cancel", call_order))
    # If we get a TimeoutError, then the code is broken.
    with pytest.raises(asyncio.CancelledError):
        await t

    return call_order


@pytest.mark.skipif(sys.version_info < (3, 11), reason="Only fixed in 3.11+")
@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_race_condition_cancel_before() -> None:
    """Test race condition when cancelling before timeout.
    If cancel happens immediately before the timeout, then
    the timeout may overrule the cancellation, making it
    impossible to cancel some tasks.
    """
    call_order = await race_condition()

    # This test is very timing dependant, so we check the order that calls
    # happened to be sure the test itself ran correctly.
    assert call_order == ["cancel", "timeout", "exit"]


@pytest.mark.skipif(sys.version_info < (3, 11), reason="Only fixed 3.11+.")
@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_race_condition_cancel_after() -> None:
    """Test race condition when cancelling after timeout.
    Similarly to the previous test, if a cancel happens
    immediately after the timeout (but before the __exit__),
    then the explicit cancel can get overruled again.
    """
    call_order = await race_condition(0.000001)

    # This test is very timing dependant, so we check the order that calls
    # happened to be sure the test itself ran correctly.
    assert call_order == ["timeout", "cancel", "exit"]
```

The reason why implement this logic catching Django Admin jQuery triggers. Django Admin using `jQuery` with  `django.jQuery` namespace. You can't reach global `jQuery` variable in original admin templates without adding an additional jQuery library. 

When the users click any "Add a new row" button on admin panel, `inlines.js` jQuery plugin triggering a custom event, which you can see implementation [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L126). The inline map widget class implementation has a handler method for add-row event. it's generating a new map widget instance along with new inline row. [Check this line. ](https://github.com/erdem/django-map-widgets/blob/master/mapwidgets/static/mapwidgets/js/mw_google_point_field_generater.js#L6)

So, widget class need to use `django.jQuery.` namespace otherwise we can't get any custom event from django admin. All admin JS implementation triggers using the same namespace.

The event triggering flow is not the main issue; the issue is inline.js loading before admin jQuery.  This wasn't happening before. If there is any static file added to [widget media](https://github.com/erdem/django-map-widgets/blob/master/mapwidgets/widgets.py#L163), `inlines.js` raising that error. Even if I use only a single JS file for widgets, the error shows up.

I am planning to load all media files after page load. That could fix the problem. If you have a better idea feel free to update the topic. Also, let me know if you get any error message during the setup process.

Sorry to chime in this late in the discussion. I think this may be closely related to #4165. As I understand the distance metric aspect here the output `coords` of `peak_local_max` is a coordinate array of the corner peaks which are then filtered with the help of cKDTree. If that is the case the proposed function `remove_near_objects` in #4165 should be able to replace this part. 

Performance wise I'm not sure which solution would be most appropriate here:
- `remove_near_objects` would require a temporary conversion of `coords` to a boolean mask (`indices=False`) and comes with additional overhead which is not needed for this use case.
- but its speed should scale significantly better with the number of peaks because most of the iteration is done in Cython. 

It could very well be that the break-even point is way beyond what is considered an expected number of peaks here so take this suggestion with a grain of salt until I or someone else had the chance to benchmark this. 

And in any case this shouldn't hold up this PR as it could be done afterwards without deprecations (if we manage to deal with the ordering of the peaks). The way I see it this is already an improvement over the old state. :+1:

The problem here is that people don't want to shutdown here.   If someone is running a procedure under interactive Python and they hit ^C they are expecting a break out of the loop or whatever is running and a return to the interactive console.  But Java is catching the ^C because it has taken over all the signal handlers.   So it takes the ^C as a signal to kill everything (which would be fine if it was in charge), and starts a shutdown sequence.  Python doesn't know anything about it and all of sudden the stack frames in Java start collapsing.  Thus a crash is likely to happen.   The correct action is for Java to take the ^C as a hint to and let Python decide if it wants to shutdown (after all it is in charge here).   If Python wants to shutdown then shutdownJVM happens and everything is normal.   

I suppose we can add a keyword argument to startJVM ``interactive=False`` which will allow ^C to proceed with the default behavior.   But 90% of the time Java shutting down without telling Python about it first is likely to just go straight to a crash.

I can't find the old comment, so here is the summary...when I was testing earlier commits here, the Azure builds for the R package were  taking 20-25  minutes, even after #2957 . So I decided to change the `R CMD check` commands for AZURE builds to not check any docs:

```
if ($env:AZURE -eq "true") {
  Write-Output "Running R CMD check without checking documentation"
  R.exe CMD check --no-multiarch --no-examples --no-manual --ignore-vignettes ${PKG_FILE_NAME} ; Check-Output $?
```

Once I did that, I was also able to save time by cutting out MiKTeX and pandoc installation. If you are running with `--no-examples --no-manual --ignore-vignettes`, you never need to create the package documentation.

I am ok with this because the docs are already checked in Linux / Mac builds and on the AppVeyor build.

Another possibility that the jobs took SO LONG to run might be based on how Azure does virtualization vs how AppVeyor does it.  I have seen that we use `/MP` [for MSVC builds](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt#L196).

From the MSVC docs for `/MP` ([docs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/build/reference/mp-build-with-multiple-processes?view=vs-2019)):

> /MP[processMax]
> If you omit the processMax argument, the compiler retrieves the number of effective processors on your computer from the operating system, and creates a process for each processor.

If you'd like, I can move any `if ($env:AZURE...` stuff out of this and into #2965 if you'd prefer. I originally didn't do that because I thought it would be complicated to remember to add it back in the right places.

`itertools.repeat()` is slightly more efficient for this use-case, because we don't care about the integers that `range()` would generate. This is [one of the recommended uses for `itertools.repeat()`.](https://stackoverflow.com/a/9098860/845210)

```python
import timeit
repeat=100_000
result1 = timeit.timeit('[random.choice(string.printable) for _ in itertools.repeat(None, 100)]', setup='import random, string, itertools', number=repeat)
print(f'Itertools took {result1:.5}')
# Itertools took 5.0574
result2 = timeit.timeit('[random.choice(string.printable) for _ in range(100)]', setup='import random, string', number=repeat)
print(f'Range took {result2:.5}')
# Range took 5.2794
```

> Out of curiosity, how do you determine when you do and do not do it? I've read about the difference, but am curious if you have any heuristics you rely on.

In this case it was because `Snapshot` already implemented `Eq`, and I didn't dig into why that's the case; maybe `PartialEq` would be enough. The distinction between `PartialEq` and `Eq` in Rust basically exists in order to support the floating point number types; IEEE 754 specifies that floating-point numbers must satisfy `NaN != NaN`, which is inconsistent with the requirement on the [Eq trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/cmp/trait.Eq.html) that `a == a` for all `a`, which `PartialEq` doesn't have. Since these types aren't floating point numbers and don't have that weird quirk of floating point numbers, I don't think the distinction matters for anything we're doing.

Re: maintenance - the goal of the states isolate the tokens from other tokens that can match similar patterns.

For instance, when parsing the `--params` option, we only want to be matching Python tokens, and not other stuff like OPT_VAL, which is very general and matches almost anything. On the same note, while parsing a non-`--params` option value, we don't need (or want) to be recognizing Python tokens and end up with a syntax error where no Python constructs are expected.

Pattern precedence is important when using regexes like that, and because some patterns overlap, it is not clear which of them should be listed (and tried) first. Lexer states thus serve as namespaces that isolate tokens groups and prevent the tokens from stepping on other unrelated  tokens' toes.

Since the call to `useTimeoutPoll` is inside this function, every time `useUserSyncStatus` is invoked it's going to create a new timer, which will not play nice with the usage tracking.

Here's a situation where this could go wrong:
1) Component A calls `useUserSyncStatus`, creates timer A
2) Since Component A is the first usage of `useUserSyncStatus`, it will call `resume()` for timer A
3) Component B calls `useUserSyncStatus`, creates timer B
4) Since Component B is the second usage, it does not call `resume()` for timer B
5) Component A is destroyed
6) Since there is still one usage by Component B (still mounted), `pause()` for either timer won't be called after Component A is destroyed 
7) Component B is destroyed
8) Since Component B was the last usage of `useUserSyncStatus`, it will call `pause()`... BUT that `pause` function refers to the timer B not timer A. Timer A is the active timer (the one we called `resume()`), and timer B never had `resume()` called, meaning timer A will continue forever.

Since the goal is to de-duplicate the polling if more than one component uses `useUserSyncStatus` and store that polling result for any usage of `useUserSyncStatus`, we only need one timer. That means calling `useTimeoutPoll(pollUserSyncStatusTask, 1000);` should exist outside of the composable function `useUserSyncStatus`. This is what I meant in my previous comment about it being at the same scope as the refs.

Make long story short:
With the `InMemoryCheckpointStore`, users can call `update_checkpoint` now even if they don't provide a checkpoint_store, and as its name shows, it keeps in memory checkpoint which would be used for internal receiver recovery from error.

----- Long story:

Previously if a user doesn't provide a `checkpoint_store`, the sdk would simply log a warning  when `update_checkpoint` called. And if error is raised during the receiving process, it would recover receiving from the starting position.

After a discussion with architects and JS team, we've decided to provide the ability to do in memory checkpoint which can be used for internal partition receiver recovery and consistent user experience on the checkpoint stuff.

Could this introduce a race condition where, e.g. two commits land on main in a short time, and the older one ends up getting deployed because the cloud build for the older one pushes the docker images to docker hub after the newer cloud build, and then the last images to get deployed are the older ones?
Or will previous jobs be cancelled, if a new commit lands?
Are there other races that could happen that I haven't thought of?
One option I can imagine is that we are explicit with version and refer to e.g. the git commit sha in the docker image version. This might not solve the races, but at least we can inspect the cluster to see which version of the images are deployed in the containers. However, we'd also then need to clean up the docker registry and not keep images around for too long, so it becomes more complicated.

So long story short, I think this could work for most of the time, but I'm concerned we could intermittently end up with the wrong version of something deployed, and perhaps a more complicated handling of version number may mitigate the issue, albeit introducing more complexity.

In this case actually all returns are error conditions - the file system URI (e.g. `foo://bar`) could not be associated to a installed filesystem, so we raise an error how to fix this.

We thus follow a progression here - pyarrow is the base and required for everything, so that is checked first. If pyarrow is installed but the URI is not found (pyarrow only supports local, s3, hdfs), the filesystem can be provided with fsspec. Thus the user should install fsspec next if it's not already there.

fsspec provides a dict `from fsspec.registry import known_implementations` which contains all fsspec-compatible filesystems that are "known" to the fsspec maintainers. Here is the current definition: https://github.com/fsspec/filesystem_spec/blob/master/fsspec/registry.py#L87

For instance, for `dropbox://something`, if it's not available, the user would get the message
```
            'DropboxFileSystem requires "dropboxdrivefs",'
            '"requests" and "dropbox" to be installed'
```

Only as a last resort we would end with the generic "Make sure to install and register your fsspec-compatible filesystem.", which should usually only come up when a user wants to use a custom URI and filesystem implementation.

IMO this order makes sense and we should keep it that way

2. The `Git` class has methods that we want to expose, so a fallible ("maybe"/"optional") wrapper type around it should probably stay simple, so that the user needs to unwrap or raise to get access.

```
$ git diff -U0

+++ b/tensorboard/data/server/lib.rs
@@ -18 +18 @@ limitations under the License.
-pub mod masked_crc;

  --> masked_crc.rs:37:1
   |
37 | const CRC_MASK_DELTA: u32 = 0xa282ead8;
   | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```

We can silence this warning—

```rust
#[allow(dead_code)]
```

```
$ git diff -U1

@@ -86 +86,3 @@ mod tests {
 }
+

$ cargo check
warning: unused manifest key: raze
warning: function is never used: `some_dead_code`
  --> masked_crc.rs:88:4
   |
88 | fn some_dead_code() {}
   |    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
   = note: `#[warn(dead_code)]` on by default

warning: 1 warning emitted

    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.02s
```

I wonder if we should make this higher?  Personally 10 has always seemed pretty low for text samples (which are generally going to not get as big as images or audio).  The multiplexer DataProvider would still of course max out at 10 samples but then at least other ones wouldn't be subject to the same constraint.

Also, hardcoding this to 10 means that the `--samples_per_plugin` flag would no longer provide an escape hatch once the DataProvider path is on by default.

In fairness, I think this is a specific instance of a more general issue which is that random sampling to a fixed _k_ steps is kind of a weird approach to use in general for the data that isn't being rendered as the entire time series at once (like scalars or histograms/distributions).  The limits we have today are more about avoiding too much memory usage in the multiplexer than really about what makes sense in terms of user experience.

But it seems like in the short term it would at least be better to make this respect `--samples_per_plugin` rather than hardcoding the default value.

Nice metaphor.

Editorial: Let me just state that I think the PVsyst-style "loss diagrams" do somewhat of a disservice, by applying the term "loss" to quantities of energy that couldn't have been generated in the first place. An example that's not a loss (IMO) is "temperature loss"; the physical reality is that cells are less efficient at higher temperature and hot air happens. There's only a "temperature loss" if the PV system could operate at lower temperature but doesn't.

If we follow the modeling process diagram, which is a guide not a rule, a module `dc_losses` could contain functions for wiring and mismatch losses, but not soiling, since soiling applies to irradiance not DC current. Debatable whether `snow_coverage` is an effect on DC current or on irradiance. 

It seems that pvlib will have multiple modules `soiling.py`, `snow.py`, `mismatch.py` or `dc_losses.py` etc. at some level, and the question is about grouping these modules into a `losses` folder, or not. @wholmgren's question suggests we should agree on a list of these modules, and maybe it will become more clear where to place them.

Here's an attempt to list categories for functions that could be broadly termed losses using my view of that term:

- irradiance reflection (iam.py)
- soiling
- snow
- shading (could overlap with DC losses)
- mismatch (cell to cell and module-to-module variation, also irradiance variation?)
- DC wiring
- inverter MPPT efficiency (not part of the current inverter models, but could be)
- tracker accuracy
- grid support functions (e.g., use of reactive power as voltage control)

`otMessage()` does not technically have a size limit. It automatically grows as new data is appended/written to it (e.g. using `otMessageAppend()`).
-  The underlying `Message` implementation will allocate and chain new buffer blocks as new data is added, but that is not visible to the users of `otMessage`. 
-  I guess the limit is technically the overall buffer pool size, but a single `otMessage` can potentially use all buffers from pool and entire pool size, so it can grow very large (also message length is `uint16_t` which limit the length to  ~65K).

https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/meshcop/commissioner.cpp#L760

`DISABLED` should be set before call `SendKeepAlive()`

There are two cases when an active commissioner can be stopped: 
* When commissioner session is rejected in KeepAlive response, the commissioner need to stop, but no need to `SendKeepAlive (reject)`. 
* When commissioner session is stopped by API, the commissioner need to send `SendKeepAlive (reject)`.

So, the commissioner can not determine if it's necessary to `SendKeepAlive()` only by checking state. `aResign` argument is required at least for the active state.

To handle things with bare integers in annotations, like `Float[32]` in `nptyping` and (presumably) [`Literal[640]` in PEP 646](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0646/#use-case-1-specifying-shape-values).

The `TypeError` here is raised because `args` is not iterable in these cases. Singletons are not an issue in other subscriptable types because they are special-cased in their handling earlier in the function.

I did the error trapping this way initially b/c EAFP model. I can think of two other ways to handle offhand at this point in the code, both of which are LBYL, but more precise:

1. Switch to a `try: iter(args); except ...` handler. Would make it more specifically catch the iterable/non-iterable difference.

2. Switch to an explicit `isinstance(args, Number)` check. This would be a very focused check, and would not cover cases with non-numeric singletons. I believe neither `nptyping` nor PEP 646 uses non-numeric singletons, but I'm not 100% sure; and, who knows what's out there in the ecosystem, or will be.

I think I'm inclined to revise to (1).

I believe that it should be possible to merge both the `invalidated` and `Errored` cases in this if-else, as neither is now persisted.

BUT: rather than "dropping" the `waiters` (from the `Running` struct), as in the first case, probably what you'll want to do is just always send the error value. The effect of dropping the `waiters` is that anyone waiting for the value of the node will get an invalidated error value, here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/17e2401e4e0a0cec602f88f1b34be9fb1b8472a6/src/rust/engine/graph/src/entry.rs#L303-L306 ... actually _sending_ them the invalidated Err value (which is what you would do if you didn't do this `result == Some(Err(N::Error::invalidated()))`) would mean that the future would be satisfied successfully with the invalidated value (ie, it would have the same effect as dropping the waiter). Not sending the Errored value makes the patch incorrectly currently.

So: all this to say: you should be able to merge the invalidated/Errored blocks as long as you actually _send_ the error value that you got (the same as how we send a successful value here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/17e2401e4e0a0cec602f88f1b34be9fb1b8472a6/src/rust/engine/graph/src/entry.rs#L498-L500).

The random wait time (once picked) will be fixed and cannot be pushed back. For example if we are at `BE=5` and pick `6 msec` as wait time, we will send the frame after `6 ms`. We can potentially receive frames during the backoff wait interval (which will be passed to next layer `Mac` but they cannot really impact the current wait time or change the current tx operation). `SubMac` layer deals with a single ongoing tx at a time and the notions of direct vs indirect come into picture at `Mac` or next layers.

The `elements` field may reference a Line or a Word (word is more common currently).

Think of a hypothetical receipt line item. The line text might be:
`1  $5.00  Slice of Pizza`

For fields Quantity, Price, and Name, respectively.

The words in that line would be:
`["1", "$5.00", "Slice", "of", "Pizza"]`

So the `Name` field would have an `elements` array of:
```json
[
  "#/readResults/0/lines/0/words/2",
  "#/readResults/0/lines/0/words/3",
  "#/readResults/0/lines/0/words/4",
]
```

If the Name referenced the entire line, it would point to extra/incorrect information related to other fields.

The primary case I was worried about was the evolving standards for `pickle`.  For example, [python 3.4 introduced version 4, and 3.8 updated to version 5 of the pickle format](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#data-stream-format), and I was worried that this update would break pickling.  At least for a simple test case of saving/loading a dict, I had no problems saving/loading between python 3.6 and 3.8 (which use different versions of the pickle spec).  I didn't try anything much more complicated (and there are some edge cases for `pickle` that can get hairy), but also we don't use `pickle` to save off super-complicated data structures and objects at this time.

So all this to say that what I was concerned about initially doesn't seem to be an issue, and yes, let's deal with how to handle this well when we start having a truly global state database we're maintaining in a cloud somewhere.

Well, we still have our database, don't we? Instead of adding to the DB only after unblocking and queuing the job, we should probably persist the request right away, but with your new `state=StateEnum.CACHED`. So we'll now have  cached and queued and running job entries. For running jobs, we agreed there is a need for some kind of timeout anyway, universally. It gets enforced by
- Processor Server model: the calling Processing Server or client CLI
- Processing Worker model: the worker instance (around `invoke_processor()`) or the processor itself (inside `process()`)

Whoever witnessed a timeout must then either resubmit or roll back upstream, i.e. push back on the queue.

The same could happen if anything goes wrong on the queue – reinstate from the DB or resubmit to the internal queue.

To me, this problem seems similar to some of the problems surrounding static initialization in C++ (e.g. https://isocpp.org/wiki/faq/ctors#static-init-order). Both to avoid the static initialization order fiasco, and to make initialization lazy, it's often best to defer initialization until the thing being initialized is used.

The best practice for this, in both C++ and Python, seems to be simply to perform the initialization in some function, so that the initialization only occurs when the function that needs it calls it. This is simple and easy to understand, and is self-documenting.

Building custom machinery around this to me sounds like trying to create a C++ wrapper around arbitrary types that avoids calling the constructor until it's actually used. Isn't it simpler just to do what the language supports directly?

Or you could put the bypass diode here using `v = v.clip(-bypass_voltage)` like [Cliff commented](#r427429840) _except note the **negative** sign_, where `bypass_voltage` would be an argument of `combine_series(..., bypass_voltage=0.5)` and 0.5[V] is a typical diode trigger voltage (_aka_ [forward voltage drop or _threshold_ voltage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diode#Forward_bias)). 

From this it's clearer that at the substring/submodule level reverse bias is not important because the substring is either bypassed or not. And the entire substring tends to behave near Vmp like the most shaded cell in the substring.

The reverse bias part of each cell is really only useful to tell us where the substring shifts from the most shaded cell to the rest of the substring, where there's a bump/jump in the current. This inflection or discontinuity occurs where the shaded cells drop into reverse bias because they can't handle the current, _ie_ after the first knee in the IV curve (usu. near Vmp, but not necessarily), the voltage of the shaded cells suddenly drops from positive to negative until the break down voltage, where the second knee starts, but unfortunately if the total breakdown voltage of the shaded cells _plus_ the forward bias of the remaining cells is less than the negative of the bypass diode trigger voltage, `-Vbypass`, then the substring is bypassed, and the voltage doesn't go any lower than `-Vbypass`.

I think we'll benefit from a little more nuance around types of failure here. The tokenserver has [some special handling](https://github.com/mozilla-services/tokenserver/blob/f19ac0e8402b8203c1db51e9321ea50cb361f634/tokenserver/views.py#L220) for subclasses of `fxa.errors.Error` and in particular for the [`TrustError` subclass](https://github.com/mozilla-services/tokenserver/blob/f19ac0e8402b8203c1db51e9321ea50cb361f634/tokenserver/views.py#L226). To play nice with tokenserver's expectations, in my head this could look roughly like:

```
try:
    for key in keys:
        try:
            resp = self._decode_key(key, token)
            break
        except jwt.exceptions.InvalidSignatureError:
            # It's only worth trying other keys in the event of `InvalidSignature`; if it was invalid for
            # other reasons (e.g. it's expired) then using a different key won't help.
            # OTOH you might prefer to let the server do a bit of extra work just to keep the control-flow simpler.
            continue
except (jwt.exceptions.DecodeError, jwt.exceptions.InvalidKeyError):
    # It wasn't a JWT at all, or it was signed using a key type we don't support.
    # Fall back to asking the FxA server to verify.
    pass
except jwt.exceptions.Error as e:
    # Any other JWT-related failure (e.g. expired token) can immediately surface as a trust error.
    raise fxa.errors.TrustError({"error": str(e)})
```

It would also be great to have tests for a couple of those different failure modes.

The post that you linked seems good. 

```
use inline instead of #define
very small functions are good candidates for inline: faster code and smaller executables (more chances to stay in the code cache)
the function is small and called very often
```

The compiler knows when to inline functions. Abusing inlines leads to code bloat, and missing stack traces when debugging. The cost overhead of a function call is very minimal, my recommendation is to only explicitly inline if you are doing something very small like returning a value or an arithmetic operation, if you have something more complex like appending to a vector, or a small loop it doesn't make any sense, does more harm than good, and again the compiler will choose when to inline. 

If you have better information on this, feel free to correct me or point to an updated source of information.

We have a code bloat problem in MXNet that surfaces on linking errors, this is partially caused by abuse of inlines like in the file you linked.

I think I see what is happening here: Even though the CodeCoverage subsystem gets initialized, none of its options are registered. This is because the subsystem is using `def register_junit_options(register, register_jvm_tool):` instead of `def register_options(cls, register):`

while the options under the latter (`register_options`) gets automatically registered, the former (`register_junit_options`) requires to be called by some other code, e.g, this is how it is called in `junit_run.py`: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/35be5116da7c98f6d789ebff9baf58a8379afdf5/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/junit_run.py#L99-L102

And when it is called by some other class,( `junit` in this example), the scope of that class gets prepended to the scope of `coverage`, thus registering the options as `--test-junit-coverage`

Consequently, setting `--scoverage-coverage=True` (scope at compile time) does not automatically set `--test-junit-coverage=True`(scope at runtime) (and vice-versa)

It first tries to parse the red locale, if it fails with `ValueError`, then it tries to get the closest match, if  it  fails (`ValueError` or `TypeError` or `babel.core.UnknownLocaleError`) or can't get a close match then it defaults to  `en_US`

Red's locale and Babel supported locale is then cached and only updated when Red's locale is changed
This avoid calling the babel parser and Try/Except blocks every single time we call `humanize_int`

Oh so there is a difference between api gateway stage and chalice stage. With chalice, it's notion of stage is just an entire separate stack of resources. So a single stage of a chalice app can only have at most one api gateway api and that api can only have one api gateway stage. By setting `varies_per_chalice_stage` it allows users to change the api gateway endpoint type per stack of resources they may have. For example, they may have a beta or dev stack/stage that they want to keep the api as a private endpoint, but a prod stack/stage that is a edge/regional endpoint. In general, chalice does not really utilize api gateway stages. I believe you really can only set the name of it.

As far as I can tell:

 1. If the **response** from the server cannot be parsed as the desired
    type, then the `response` property of the XHR is `null` (at least
    when `responseType` is `"json"` on Chrome M73 on Linux).

 2. If one tries to set the `responseType` **property** is to an invalid
    response type (like `"json"` in IE11), then this property is not
    changed from its previous value. In particular, in IE11, it is not
    possible to create an XHR such that `req.responseType === "json"`.

The first point isn’t really what I’m interested in. I’m concerned that
the check `req.responseType == 'json'` will always evaluate to `false`
in IE, which means that control will never flow into the very block
that’s supposed to handle IE compatibility.

It’s totally plausible that I’m wrong about some or all of this; the XHR
spec and implementations are certainly not my expertise.

That class is an ugly hack to enable the log handling in the scenario player. 
The stdlib logging module's base handler used to transparently call `write()` on whatever the formatter output as the `record`. 

The SP 'abuses' that by passing the raw `LogRecord` instances to a fake stream object in order to render the log into the urwid pane. (see `scenario_player.ui::NonStringifyingProcessorFormatter / _DummyFormatter`)

In Python 3.7 an optimization was added to logging that concatenates the terminator (usually `\n`) to the record before calling write. This class simply returns the other argument.

Well, I blindly inherited this logic from #5494, and now it seems that it's incorrect. There's indeed a difference in how boolean and non-boolean binary operations treat columns mismatching.

At the first glance, there are 3 types of behavior in pandas:
1. Union columns
2. Raise an error if columns are not fully matching
3. Apply a binary operation to an intersection of columns and fill the rest with NaNs

Here's how each bin op behaves in pandas:
```
combine_first: union of columns in the result

eq : failure if columns don't fully match
ge
gt
le
lt
ne

__and__ : the result is an intersection of columns + nan columns for the rest
__or__
__xor__
... other non-boolean methods
```

So, apparently, not all boolean operations go through the 'union' way, I'll check how to align modin's logic with pandas

This is a non-guaranteed way to do cleanup in a test, because if there's an error or assertion failure in the test, `os.remove` will not run.

You've got a few options for how to make it better:
1. Wrap the entire test in try/finally and put os.remove in the finally block.
2. Wrap the entire test in `with temporary_dir() as path:` and make sure you create the file inside the `path` directory (the temporary_dir context manager takes care of deleting the directory when it exits)
3. Create a separate fixture to handle the file:

```
@pytest.fixture
def org_file():
    with open(ORG_FILE, "w") as file:
        file.write(ORG_FILE_TEXT)
    try:
        yield  # this is where the test using the fixture runs
    finally:
        os.remove(ORG_FILE)
```

I like option 3 because then you don't have to repeat the logic for each test that uses the fixture.

I'll go through my thinking on this, and please point out any flaws.

Uuid5 generates different uuids for different seeds, the seed in this PR being both the file's directory and a static uuid.  So `uuid.uuid5(arg1, arg2) = uuid_a` and `uuid.uuid5(arg1, arg3) = uuid_b`.  The different is that if `uuid.uuid5(arg1, arg2) = uuid_a` occurs again, it will generate the same `uuid_a` output.

```
>>> from uuid import uuid5, UUID
>>> uuid5(UUID('6a56ca02-b6f0-4c1a-a4b0-fb0068ce80ad'), '/tmp/1')
UUID('e3e49c59-338c-5369-a215-3d68bda76d69')
>>> uuid5(UUID('6a56ca02-b6f0-4c1a-a4b0-fb0068ce80ad'), '/tmp/2')
UUID('48220b67-6c36-500b-ae08-ecfa48951864')
>>> uuid5(UUID('6a56ca02-b6f0-4c1a-a4b0-fb0068ce80ad'), '/tmp')
UUID('98da1ac5-114d-539b-b727-948e7754ec95')
>>> uuid5(UUID('6a56ca02-b6f0-4c1a-a4b0-fb0068ce80ad'), '/tmp/1')
UUID('e3e49c59-338c-5369-a215-3d68bda76d69')
```

The idea being that all pre-existing paths cannot conflict since they co-existed together peacefully in the first place.

So files:

```
/tmp/a/b.txt
/tmp/b/a.txt
/tmp/b/b.txt
/tmp/c/b.txt
```

would map to these inside of the container:

```
uuid_a/b.txt
uuid_b/a.txt
uuid_b/b.txt
uuid_c/b.txt
```

> So why not skip to the directory name itself?

If I understand your question correctly, we could use the same directory names as the files originally had, but they might conflict with folders that already exist within the container.

> Or use it to see the uuid5 generator?

If I understand this to be to "seed" the uuid5 generator with the directory, I'm using `os.path.dirname(fob['location']))` to seed the uuid5.  Though I'm not sure if that's the question here.

> What if there are location URIs that end in the same directory name but are otherwise unrelated? This could lead to files overwriting each other. Or files being adjacent to one another when they should not be.

I was wondering about this, and was trying to reason if this could happen if the input files were writable in a an input dir outside of the workdir.  I haven't tested this.

I don't think this is correct. You are essentially
```
read(file.json) -> convert it to bytearray -> write(file.cpp, bytearray)
```
which is in the end operation that just copies the contents from the json files to cpp files. Bytearray is just a different representation of the same data. What we want is to **preprocess** the json data to a valid C++ language constructs, that can be then used from the C++ yaramod without even accessing the filesystem.

But in your case, if you open generated .cpp files, they are just plain json objects.

However, we have some limitations to keep yaramod portable:
- you must use a construct that allows for "literals" with arbitrary size, as we discussed windows compilers can impose a limitation on a length of a string literal

For example you should be able to use arrays, choosing a proper type is up to you:
```cpp
char MATH_MODULE[] = ['{', '\n', '\t', 'k', 'i', 'n', 'd', ':', ...]
```
then in the `module-pool` you would need to convert it to some type that is compatible with your json library. Maybe using `reinterpret_cast` or some other `*_cast` or doing it by "manually" with C++ function for example. 

After the conversion you should have access to the original `file.json`. I know this might sound complicated but we are doing this to ensure cross platform compatibility and users of our library won't have to do anything to adapt with those changes.

If we introduce a wrapper like this:
```python
class Path(datatype([('underlying_path', text_type)]):
  pass
```

then in rust every place we currently:
```rust
let prefix = externs::project_str(&request, "prefix");
```

we'd need to instead:
```rust
let prefix_wrapper = externs::project_ignoring_type(&request, "prefix");
let prefix = externs::project_str(&prefix_wrapper, "underlying_path");
```

It would also mean that on the rust side, every time we want to return a path, we'd need to call an additional python function; i.e.
```rust
let value = externs::store_utf8(s);
```

would become:
```rust
let string_value = externs::store_utf8(s);
let wrapper_value = externs::unsafe_call(&core.types.construct_path_wrapper, &[string_value]);
```

I suspect that the runtime overhead of doing this would be noticeable, but probably not hugely significant.


We could do the less involved thing of just having a `PathWrapper = text_type` and referring to the thing as `PathWrapper` instead of `text_type`, but we'd not get any value other than documentation here; nothing would enforce that the correct thing is used.

I'm proposing that we only look at the global `[autoflake]` subsystem and do not consult individual targets. Well, user is not supposed to be working on those refs.
Just as users are not supposed to work on `manifest_rev` branch.

Those are solely intended for `west` usage.
If github had allowed fetching the SHA directly, then there would be no need for fetching the whole ref-space locally.
The `refs/west_refs/*` is not linked to any git remote, and content will change with a changing url in manifest.
Thus, if user need this kind of functionality, the safest for them will be to create their own own remote, and fetch into that.

And in such case, they are probably way beyond a simple `west update` of a SHA and instead doing specific work, where they have local branches as well as a need to do `git push` to a fork.

So I don't think we should make short refs names, in order to aid the user.
Also more unique name, such as `west_refs` minimizes the (I agree, potentially small) risk of collision.

This notification is only made if the caller has already acquired the GIL, and allows the python code to override those functions to get immediate notification of completion. (It can then do some work for that immediately on the IOThread itself, though anything substantial likely requires it to use a multiprocessing.Queue or similar to coordinate back to the main thread. Or a worker of its own.)

Or the python can just ignore them (in which case those two calls above are no-ops) and poll for completion on the main thread. (Though it should use the new  complete() method rather than polling received() as in the current example code - that has problems distinguishing between actual completion, partial completion, completion with no results, and error.)

Or it can call wait() which I have now exposed for a non-polling solution.

It's a matter of taste and what fits with the rest of the client's implementation.

In the majority of cases, unsafe wraps "memory safety" cases... in those cases, using unsafe can cause segfaults (which are generally worse than panics, because you don't get a backtrace).

In _this_ case, the unsafety is around file handles, and generally deals with files being closed early or multiple times. Rather than memory safety, that will "just" cause operations on those file handles to fail. But since any meaningful operation on a file handle involves a syscall, they are all already fallible.

The case that could happen here is that we could try to open a file handle for a "bogus" file. But the consequence of that is an IO error the first time you deal with it ("file is closed", etc).

Just to give you some insight nonetheless, here is the logic for RPM distributions:
1) Notice that we are on a RedHat-based system because of `/etc/redhat-release` existence.
2) Check if we are on a very old distribution, which is implied by Python 2.6 availability
2.a) Check if we can enable SCL in a consistent way: if yes, prepare Python3 bootstrap for legacy OSes
2.b) If not, prepare to not upgrade the system, and run Certbot without upgrade if non interactive, to avoid breaking cert renewal on these pathologic cases
3) Check we are on very recent systems (Fedora 29, RHEL 8, CentOS 8) that are deprecating Python 2.7: if yes, prepare new Python 3 bootstrap
4) Fallback to install the classic Python 2.7 bootstrap

>  logger must be explicitly activated

Python's default configuration includes [a "handler of last resort"](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.lastResort) which writes messages of level WARNING or higher to the stderr. Thus, the warnings here _will_ be shown unless the message is processed by an explicitly registered handler that directs it elsewhere. In the case our tutorials/examples, there are no other handlers registered. In the case of beamlines, I believe that WARNING and higher are shown in the stderr and INFO and higher are logged to a file.

We generally avoid printing because there is no way for downstream libraries to silence prints.

Some explanation of my design decisions, though I'm happy to make any changes you'd prefer:

The `self._session` variable is how the transaction persists between calls to `execute` when the user wants to handle transactions themselves without autocommit. If we don't store the session then the transaction will either end up as a leaked database connection (if we don't close it) or will end up closed (making the user's subsequent queries worthless).

The reason the session is closed at the end of every `execute` call in autocommit is because often SQLAlchemy sessions have the same scope as transactions, so they're closed at the end of a transaction. This is just replicating that convention, but can be changed to keep the session open if desired -- the `__del__` method and Flask teardown function ensure the session will close eventually.

As for why I used `session` instead of `self._session` for execution, that's largely based on the existing codebase before these changes, where `connection` and `self._connection` are different things -- one stores the persistent connection, the other is used for execution in this specific call.

As for using a flag for `self._in_transaction`, that can certainly be done; I just thought it was repetitive and that adding an additional instance variable to the class was probably not the best design decision given the design decisions made by previous contributors (especially when a lot of the existing code does something very similar to checking if a variable is None, as by checking if the class hasattr). But this can obviously be added, and we could just use `self._session` (and even keep the session open), if preferred.

JPype misbehaves a bit in that it   automatically wraps to the most derived class even if that class should be unavailable due to the module system. Can I suggest another solution, which sends the really big subscription message in smaller frames? Instead of sending multiple smaller subscription messages.
```python3
msg['action'] = 'subscribe'
bs = msgpack.packb(msg)
frames = ( bs[i:i+self._max_frame_size] for i in range(0, len(bs), self._max_frame_size) )
await self._ws.send(frames)
```

I got this from the [documentation](https://websockets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/client.html#websockets.client.WebSocketClientProtocol.send): 
>[send()](https://websockets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/client.html#websockets.client.WebSocketClientProtocol.send) also accepts an iterable or an asynchronous iterable of strings, bytestrings, or bytes-like objects to enable [fragmentation](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6455.html#section-5.4). Each item is treated as a message fragment and sent in its own frame. All items must be of the same type, or else [send()](https://websockets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/client.html#websockets.client.WebSocketClientProtocol.send) will raise a [TypeError](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#TypeError) and the connection will be closed.

I read about it in `FastDDS` docs and they say:
```
events are always notified to the most specific Entity Listener that can handle the event, callbacks that DomainParticipantListener inherits from other Listeners will only be called if no other Entity was able to handle the event
```

It looks like it's possible to do all the work inside the participant listener but it has some disadvantages:
It will call all the callbacks and they will have to decide if the reader match their writer (topic name / QOS), and it will happen for each topic we have, meaning irrelevant CPU time consumption and locks even when the new reader is not for the topic we want to read/write.
I also spoke with @OhadMeir and we think a specific listener is more efficient here.

And, before I push any more code - I think the new plan will be to align with the official Swift terminology - which actually brings this backend closer to Go.

https://www.swift.org/package-manager/#conceptual-overview
> Swift organizes code into modules. Each module specifies a namespace and enforces access controls on which parts of that code can be used outside of the module.

> A package consists of Swift source files and a manifest file. The manifest file, called Package.swift, defines the package’s name and its contents using the PackageDescription module.

So, that would mean changing `tailor` rules to create a `swift_package` in the folders which have a `Package.swift` and then all of the source files are bundled into modules. 

Swift then uses "products" to mean libraries and executables - but it's probably perfectly fair to stick with `swift_library` and `swift_binary` or `swift_executable` as that's what products actually group together into one concept.

this is an unfortunate little tidbit. When a user provides a resource def directly to an asset, we can't know their intent. In order to make it possible to use provided resources directly within the op, we make them required on the op itself. This means that if I provide a resource required by my io manager, and then remove all resources, I still require the key transitively required by the io manager. If we had a notion of optional resources, we could default to making provided resource defs optional resource keys, so that it wouldn't be necessary to re-provide that value.

```
@mx.util.use_np
def test_amp_concat():
  class TestNet(nn.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self):
      super(TestNet, self).__init__()
      self.fc1 = nn.Dense(16)
      self.fc2 = nn.Dense(16)

    def forward(self, x):
      x1 = self.fc1(x)
      x2 = self.fc2(x)
      x = mx.np.concat((x1, x2), axis=1)
      return x

  net = TestNet()
  net.initialize()

  data_example = mx.np.random.uniform(-1, 1, (1, 16))

  exp_data = mx.symbol.Variable('data')
  exp_amp_data = mx.symbol.amp_cast(exp_data, dtype=AMP_DTYPE)

  exp_weight = [mx.symbol.Variable(f"fc{i}_weight") for i in range(2)]
  exp_bias = [mx.symbol.Variable(f"fc{i}_bias") for i in range(2)]
  exp_fc = [mx.symbol.FullyConnected(exp_amp_data, exp_weight[i], exp_bias[i], num_hidden=1)
            for i in range(2)]
  exp_sym = mx.symbol.Concat(*exp_fc)
  exp_sym = mx.symbol.amp_cast(exp_sym, dtype='float32')
  exp_sym = exp_sym.get_backend_symbol(SG_PASS_NAME)
  check_amp_fuse(net, [data_example], exp_sym)

  exp_fc[0] = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(exp_amp_data, exp_weight[0], exp_bias[0], num_hidden=1)
  exp_fc[1] = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(exp_data, exp_weight[1], exp_bias[1], num_hidden=1)
  exp_sym = mx.symbol.Concat(*exp_fc)
  exp_sym = exp_sym.get_backend_symbol(SG_PASS_NAME)
  check_amp_fuse(net, [data_example], exp_sym, ['sg_onednn_fully_connected_1'])
```

Thanks @jwhui  for reviewing this. I'd like to ask for your help checking something.

In this commit/PR, I removed the `NetworkData` version of ` PrefixMatch` and used the new helper methods where it was being used before.

The `NetworkData::PrefixMatch()` calculates the `matchedLength` and if larger than given `aLength` it would return the `matchedLength` which can potentially be larger than `aLength`.

There is one place that removing this may change the existing behavior. 

- In `LeaderBase::ExternalRouteLookup()` we go through all the `PrefixTlv` from network data and try to match them with a given `aDestination` address and keep track of the best match so far.
- I changed the code in this PR to use `Address::MatchPrefix()` and then use the prefix length itself as the match length.
- This would be different from `NetworkData::PrefixMatch` which can potentially use a longer match length than  prefix length. 
- I think the new model is better and correct but just want to double-check with you as well.

```python
    field_names: set[str] = set()
    class_vars: set[str] = set()

            field_names.update(getattr(base, 'model_fields', {}).keys())
            class_vars.update(base.__class_vars__)
            private_attributes.update(base.__private_attributes__)
    return field_names, class_vars, private_attributes
```

I would actually like to get this project enabled with setuptools_scm, but until the project itself can adopt setuptools_scm, it seems a bad idea to paper over the issue that `setup.py --version` is reporting an incorrect version by partially adopting setuptools_scm but only in docs.

Currently, setuptools defines `egg_info.tag_build` and `egg_info.tag_date` to provide a post-release tag. I'm a little surprised that `setup.py --version` (what jaraco.packaging currently uses to elicit the version) doesn't include that tag. That's probably another bug.

Regardless, `jaraco.packaging` should probably invoke PEP 517 to get the package metadata and the version from that. Doing so would have the unfortunate side-effect of rendering the `.post` and date even for tagged commits (which are [only disabled when cutting a release](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/b5fa6ad11e1344648b470ff0d847a7d940b4c99d/tox.ini#L67-L68)).

Since adopting setuptools_scm for this project would solve all of these problems, I'd like to first tackle that issue (#2656).

So to investigate this I wrote a simple eval script, which I pushed to <https://github.com/1e100/mnasnet_trainer/blob/master/eval.py>.

The results with Pillow-SIMD/libjpeg-turbo-8 are as follows:
```
Dev/mnasnet_trainer % ./eval.py
Evaluating pretrained mnasnet1_0
1.0769143749256522 [('prec1', 73.490265), ('prec5', 91.53294)]
Evaluating pretrained mnasnet0_5
1.3720299355229553 [('prec1', 67.59815), ('prec5', 87.51842)]
```
Neither of which matches the published numbers exactly. MNASNet 1.0 is slightly worse than in the doc says. MNASNet 0.5 is slightly better than the checkpoint name would imply (67.598% top1 vs 67.592).

The results with "plain" pillow 6.0.0 from PyPl are as follows:
```
% ./eval.py
Evaluating pretrained mnasnet1_0
1.0772113243536072 [('prec1', 73.46037), ('prec5', 91.52099)]
Evaluating pretrained mnasnet0_5
1.372453243756781 [('prec1', 67.606026), ('prec5', 87.50845)]
```
So the top1 for 1.0 gets a bit worse, and for 0.5 it gets a bit better. I've observed such sensitivity with other "efficient" models in the past. In particular, the resize algorithm (which is different in Pillow SIMD) seems to make a noticeable difference. The effect of this on smaller models is easily measurable. Something as mundane as a different JPEG decoder affects them, and so do the software versions: CUDA/cuDNN, PyTorch, etc - a number of these are different between then and now.

Just to be on the safe side, though, I kicked off another run for 1.0. I should have the results sometime over the weekend, one way or the other.

```python
from typing import Literal, get_args

# `Literal` is just an annotation to tell the user what option they can input.
# It's not a type, not a data structure.
KEEP_OPS = Literal["all", "first", "last", None, 1, "OrAnyOtherOption"]

def func(keep: KEEP_OPS="all"):
    # need to check first by us
    # there are some runtime 3rd packages to check use inputting and avoid us manually checking
    # but they are all in the developing stage.
    if keep not in get_args(KEEP_OPS):
        raise ValueError()

    if keep == 'all':  # more readable than Enum
        # do something

    return keep

# What you give to `keep`, that `keep` is you what gives.
func('all')  # -> 'all'
func(None)  # -> None
```

```suggestion
        if status.code in (101, 304, 400):
```

You convinced me at one point that Python is smart enough to avoid the performance penalty that would come from constructing the tuple of constants just to see if some value is in it. That probably only applies to tuples of constants, not mutable types like a set (though it could, if a static code analyzer sees that this set doesn't escape the expression). Also, 3 is a small enough number of items that the hashmap-lookup might even be slower than iterating over the options.

But, of course, the main reason is for consistency. All of the other "is this a member of?" cases are tuples, not sets.

@ericl Since the actor pool is static and I'm trying to keep this first-pass pool simple, we currently don't wait until the actor is ready to add it to the pool. I think that this could lead to liveness issues, e.g. if a map task is submitted to an actor that will never start due to resource constraints, while there are other (running) actors available.

There seem to be a few possible solutions:
1. Incorporate the `actor.ready.remote()` futures into the executor control loops.
2. Add a control loop to the execution state or actor pool submitter, similar to the `ActorPoolStrategy` control loop.
3. Truncate the pool size to the available resources in the cluster to get a soft assurance of liveness.
4. When picking an actor, choose the actor with that smallest task queue that is also running.
5. At `ActorPool.pick_actor()` time (i.e. task submission time), block until an actor is running.

I think that a combination of (4) and (5) could work well for this PR, with (3) being a nice-to-have, and (1) or (2) being the eventual solution needed for the streaming executor. What do you think?

There is no effect on the values produced by masking procedure (since the disparity is rescaled as well, not only the image). Slight rotations and shifts are added only to the original images and not to the disparity maps or masks, therefore they remain unscathed throughout the procedure.

Since this thresholded valid mask is computed from the resized bilinear interpolation of the disparity values that are converted to floating point values, an original pixel  if beneath `max_disparity * rescale_scale` will still lie end up being masked.

In terms of definitions, having the disparity at the end is more of a "I want the network to be able to output only disparities bellow a certain threshold".

For testing it was "I want to evaluate my raw-input disparities only up to some threshold that I know".

@clarkzinzow it seems like we already call `prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE)` in `prepare_dataset_shard`

```

@PublicAPI(stability="beta")
def prepare_dataset_shard(tf_dataset_shard: tf.data.Dataset):
    """A utility function that overrides default config for Tensorflow Dataset.

    This should be used on a TensorFlow ``Dataset`` created by calling
    ``iter_tf_batches()`` on a ``ray.data.Dataset`` returned by
    ``ray.train.get_dataset_shard()`` since the dataset has already been sharded
    across the workers.

    Args:
        tf_dataset_shard (tf.data.Dataset): A TensorFlow Dataset.

    Returns:
        A TensorFlow Dataset with:
            - autosharding turned off
            - prefetching turned on with autotune enabled
    """
    options = tf.data.Options()
    options.experimental_distribute.auto_shard_policy = (
        tf.data.experimental.AutoShardPolicy.OFF
    )
    return tf_dataset_shard.with_options(options).prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE)
```

There is one problem with `az.ps1` for `pip`. 

- ESRP code-sign was executed on Azure DevOps's Windows agent. When `git` checks out the source code on Windows, the end of line sequence is `CRLF` (`\r\n`). This is the default of Windows `git`:
   ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4003950/185730246-608b7fcd-fa45-42fa-88bb-eed867b487af.png)
   so the signature was generated for the `CRLF` version of `az.ps1`. 
- However, wheels are built on Linux agent, converting `az.ps1`'s end of line sequence to `LF` (`\n`). 
- Even when installing with `pip -e`, `pip` converts `CRLF` to `LF` (for simplicity I guess).

Thus, the signature doesn't match the file content anymore.

The existing logic detects venv through pyenv only when a `.python-version` file exists in the current directory.

In some projects, I've seen multiple repos use a single `.python-version` in the parent directory. However, it will still behave the same if the file is located in the current path as in the previous version.

`value_counts(sort=False)` produces Series sorted by indices (indices contains counted values) when `sort=True` sorts the resulted Series by values (frequencies of the counted values):
```python
>>> pandas.Series([1, 2, 2, 2, 2, -1, -1, 1]).value_counts(sort=False)
-1    2
 1    2
 2    4
dtype: int64
>>> pandas.Series([1, 2, 2, 2, 2, -1, -1, 1]).value_counts(sort=True)
 2    4
-1    2
 1    2
dtype: int64
```
So `not sort` argument for groupby says:
1. If `sort=False` (so the indices of the result must be sorted) ask groupby to sort the indices, so we don't need to do it in a separate query further
2. If `sort=True` (so the values of the result must be sorted in a separate query) ask groupby to not sort anything in order to speed up the groupby phase.

Python's built-in type `int` is immutable, it's okay to use `x += y`. Statement `x += y` will create a new `int` instance.

```python
>>> x = 1000

>>> x
1000
>>> id(x)
140102686402576

>>> x += 1
>>> x
1001
>>> id(x)
140102686403152
```

Here `l` and `sum_` are tensors, `sum_ += l` will not create a new instance. `slice_map.append((i, sum_))` is adding the reference to the same instance `sum_` in memory.

```python
>>> import torch
>>> sum_ = 0
>>> l = torch.ones((), dtype=torch.int32)

>>> sum_
0
>>> id(sum_)
94230645960128

>>> sum_ += l
>>> sum_
tensor(1, dtype=torch.int32)
>>> id(sum_)
140100675095104

>>> sum_ += l
>>> sum_
tensor(2, dtype=torch.int32)
>>> id(sum_)
140100675095104
```

That is a good question. I had always followed the rule of thumb to use a context manager when opening a file to be safe. It should not make a difference in virtually all cases - the file will be closed on garbage collection and having the file open doesn't stop other processes from reading or modifying the file. However, if someone is using Windows and going to delete the file before garbage collection (or thinks another process might delete it), then the file must be explicitly closed before it can be deleted. So I think to be safe, it is ever so slightly better to use a context manager.

```python
import h5py
import os

filename = 'nwbfile.nwb'
f = h5py.File(filename, 'r')
os.remove(filename)  # fails on windows
``` 

See also:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56149237/do-i-need-to-manually-close-a-hdf5-file
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7395542/is-explicitly-closing-files-important/7395906#7395906

> For generic functionality, enums (or booleans where applicable) should be preferred, as those can be type-checked statically. 
I agree with bools

As for `enums`:
* Using single `technical` enum for a series/all device, where half of the values are N/A in the device `A` and other half ar N/A in the device `B` (and results in runtime exception) doesn't bring much profit in terms of type checking. No profit because correct types will result in run time error when using the unsupported value from enums. So just a set of supported string names (that could be requested in API for each device) doesn't differ form enum in such case.

* Maybe the main profit of enum is for internationalization, e.g. we could consider aggregated `enum` for all states of all `airpurifier` devices just as a list of all known states names, some of them are unique, some of them a reused in multiple devices. For new device `C` a contributor could reuse existing states and add new states if required. So we could implement translation for these states instead of doing it for each device personally, e.g. to avoid cases when `A` uses `"level #1"` and `B` uses `"Level1"` default names instead of `AllStates.LEVEL_1`. So each device should provide list of supported states from the enum and some mapping used for device response parsing from incoming object to one of supported enum states.


>`shuii.humidifier.jsq*`  should be implemented using a simple class that adapts to the model-specific differences. This means sharing the same data classes (for status) and raising exceptions when unavailable methods are called.

To my mind `shuii.humidifier.jsq001` and `shuii.humidifier.jsq002` are two very different devices and the most similar in them is just their device names. Their feature sets somewhat overlaps, but there is a difference on both sides, also there is a difference in communication protocols, command names, response format, state names, state ranges and their ids. Seems we need some generic humidifier/purifier API that could cover a wide set of features, and each device could report what features are supported from the all known features set.

`scikit-learn`'s documentation is very c learn about this: validation should not happen at initialization time.

From [the "initialization" section in "Developing scikit-learn estimators"](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/developers/develop.html#instantiation):

> There should be no logic, not even input validation, and the parameters should not be changed.

And from [the "parameters and init" section](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/developers/develop.html#parameters-and-init)

> The easiest and recommended way to accomplish this is to not do any parameter validation in `__init__`. All logic behind estimator parameters, like translating string arguments into functions, should be done in `fit`.

I'd say that this is a realistic use case. Consider this scenario: a library author wrote a generic base class, but users of this library may not be that proficient with typing (or they chose to not type annotate stuff at all), so they inherit from the generic base class without specifying the type. I've seen this quite a lot actually.

[PEP 484](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/#user-defined-generic-types) also states:
> Subclassing a generic class without specifying type parameters assumes `Any` for each position.

So I think it's important that we support this use case in `cloudpickle` as well.
